risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "We designed the study to randomly compare the two groups, and randomisation was carried out using sealed envelopes." Comment: The paper states that participants were randomly compared and mentions sealed envelopes, but this describes the allocation mechanism/concealment rather than how the random sequence itself was generated. No explicit random sequence generation method (e.g. computer-generated list, random numbers, blocks, stratification, coin toss) is reported.

Quote: "We designed the study to randomly compare the two groups, and randomisation was carried out using sealed envelopes." Comment: The paper mentions envelopes, but does not specify that they were sequentially numbered, opaque, and sealed. Because the concealment mechanism is not explicitly described in enough detail to show that recruiters could not foresee the next assignment, allocation concealment is unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Fraction of inspired oxygen was manually adjusted in the control group, which is the traditional routine for neonatal care in our NICU, and in the other group, it was controlled by the Auto-Mixer®." and "In the control group, the bedside healthcare providers manually adjusted the FiO2 in line with our standard NICU practice... In the treatment group, the SpO2 values were received by the Auto-Mixer®, which adjusted the FiO2 as previously described." Comment: The intervention and control procedures were visibly different, so personnel delivering care were not blinded, and participants/parents would also likely have been aware of the device-based intervention. The main outcomes (time within target SpO2 range, nursing interventions, oxygen received) are directly influenced by caregiver behavior and management decisions, so lack of blinding could plausibly affect outcomes.

Quote: "Oxygen saturation was continuously measured in each infant using the monitor with signal extraction technology... which could automatically identify motion-associated SpO2 artefacts and exclude them from analysis." and "During the 12-h period of the study, we recorded the SpO2 and FiO2 values for each infant in real time, using a computer set-up for this purpose." Comment: The outcome assessor was primarily a lab/imaging/automated monitoring system (pulse oximeter/computer recording), not a person making subjective judgements. Reported outcomes in this trial were objective physiological and process measures (percentage of time within SpO2 target, time above 95%, average SpO2/FiO2, manual interventions). Although blinding of staff/therapist is not described, these automated measurements are unlikely to be influenced by knowledge of allocation, and no subjective self-reported or clinician-rated outcomes were reported in this study period.

Quote: "Ten infants were randomised to each group and completed the 12-h study period... We recorded data for 720 min for each infant, and this provided 7200 min on the 10 infants with routine manual adjustments and 7200 min on the 10 newborns in the Auto-Mixer® group." Comment: Post-randomization follow-up for the analysed 12-hour outcomes was complete in both arms (10/10 analysed in each group; 0% attrition, no arm imbalance). The eight not included were pre-randomization exclusions (parental refusal or investigators unavailable) and do not count as attrition for this domain. Although some SpO2 signal artefacts/data dropouts occurred within monitoring, the participant-level outcome data were still recorded for the full 12-hour period in all randomized infants.
